one_line_takeaway: SAMA shows that diffusion language models' bidirectional multi-mask querying creates a much larger membership-inference attack surface than autoregressive models, and a sign-based robust aggregation over progressively denser masks exploits it to roughly 30% relative AUC improvement (and up to ~8x TPR at low FPR) over the best of twelve prior MIA baselines.
key_finding: On the six MIMIR splits against LLaDA-8B-Base, SAMA achieves AUC of 0.850 (ArXiv), 0.876 (GitHub), 0.657 (HackerNews), 0.814 (PubMed Central), 0.790 (Wikipedia(en)), and 0.778 (Pile CC), versus every baseline clustering near AUC ~0.48-0.60 (best baseline, Ratio, reaches 0.597/0.743/0.575/0.555/0.653/0.604 respectively on those same datasets). Averaged across datasets the paper reports SAMA AUC of 0.81 vs. the best baseline (Ratio) at 0.62 (~30% relative AUC improvement), and at TPR@1%FPR SAMA reaches 0.16 on average vs. 0.04 for the best baseline (~4x improvement, described elsewhere in the paper as up to 8x at low FPR). On GitHub specifically (highest memorization), SAMA attains AUC 0.876 with TPR@10%FPR 0.647 vs. Ratio's 0.743 AUC / 0.355 TPR@10%FPR. Traditional autoregressive-model MIAs perform near-random (AUC ~0.50) on DLMs, and diffusion-specific MIAs ported from the image domain (SecMI, PIA) barely exceed AUC 0.52, confirming neither prior AR-MIA techniques nor prior image-diffusion MIA techniques transfer to DLMs. An ablation (Figure 3, MIMIR datasets) starting from a plain loss attack (AUC ~0.5) shows: +reference-model calibration gives 0.09-0.19 AUC gain; +progressive masking adds a further 2-3%; +robust sign-based subset aggregation is the dominant contributor, adding 20-30% AUC; +adaptive weighting adds a final 3-5%.
core_contribution: The paper presents SAMA (Subset-Aggregated Membership Attack), the first systematic membership inference attack (MIA) targeting fine-tuned Diffusion Language Models (DLMs, e.g., LLaDA, Dream). The authors analyze why DLMs' bidirectional, multi-configuration masking creates more MIA attack surface than autoregressive models' single fixed left-to-right prediction pattern, then design an attack that exploits this: (1) progressive masking across increasing densities (T=16 steps from 5% to 50% masking), (2) robust subset aggregation that samples N=128 random subsets of m=10 masked positions per step and converts each subset's loss difference (reference model loss minus target model loss) into a binary sign indicator rather than using raw magnitudes (motivated by empirically observed heavy-tailed noise from domain-adaptation effects overwhelming simple loss averaging), and (3) adaptive inverse-step weighting that upweights sparser-mask steps, which the authors find give cleaner signals, when combining the per-step scores into a final membership score.
threat_model: Grey-box adversary against a fine-tuned target DLM (M^T_DF): can query the model with arbitrary custom-masked inputs and receive per-position predictive probabilities/logits, but cannot access model parameters, gradients, or activations. The adversary also has grey-box query access to a reference model (M^R_DF), ideally the pre-trained base model from which the target was fine-tuned (or an alternative reference model when unavailable, discussed in their appendix). This mirrors real deployment where fine-tuned weights are released alongside their public base models, and the paper notes LLaDA's public in-filling demo interfaces already support the custom masked-input queries the attack requires. Goal: given a candidate text sequence x, output a binary/scored prediction of whether x was in the target model's fine-tuning training set.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: SAMA specifically targets mask-predict diffusion models, and its effectiveness on other diffusion paradigms (e.g., continuous or non-masking discrete diffusion) is unexplored. The attack requires a reference model with compatible tokenizer and masking scheme to the target (their Appendix D.6 discusses implications when an ideal same-family reference is unavailable). The progressive-masking component is specialized to DLMs' bidirectional structure and is not directly portable to autoregressive or standard masked language models, though the authors suggest the sign-based robust aggregation idea alone could generalize to AR/MLM MIAs as future work. Evaluations against explicit privacy defenses (differential privacy, LoRA fine-tuning) are relegated to an appendix (Section D.5) not captured in the extracted main-body text reviewed here. The main results table shown covers LLaDA-8B-Base only for the six MIMIR splits; Dream-v0-7B-Base and the three NLP-benchmark datasets (WikiText-103, AG News, XSum) are reported only in an appendix (Section D.3) not reviewed in full here.

datasets_used: MIMIR benchmark: ArXiv, GitHub, HackerNews, PubMed Central, Wikipedia(en), Pile CC domain splits
quantitative_results: SAMA AUC on LLaDA-8B-Base: ArXiv 0.850, GitHub 0.876, HackerNews 0.657, PubMed Central 0.814, Wikipedia(en) 0.790, Pile CC 0.778
Best baseline (Ratio) AUC on same datasets: ArXiv 0.597, GitHub 0.743, HackerNews 0.575, PubMed Central 0.555, Wikipedia(en) 0.653, Pile CC 0.604
Average SAMA AUC 0.81 vs. best baseline (Ratio) average AUC 0.62, a ~30% relative improvement
Average TPR@1%FPR: SAMA 0.16 vs. best baseline 0.04 (~4x); paper also reports up to 8x improvement at low FPR thresholds
GitHub dataset: SAMA TPR@10%FPR 0.647 (AUC 0.876) vs. Ratio TPR@10%FPR 0.355 (AUC 0.743)
All other AR-model baselines (Loss, ZLIB, Lowercase, Min-K%, Min-K%++, BoWs, ReCall, CON-ReCall, Neighbor) cluster at AUC ~0.48-0.66 across datasets, near-random
Diffusion-specific baselines ported from image domain (SecMI, PIA) achieve AUC no higher than ~0.525 on any of the six MIMIR splits

Ablation (MIMIR datasets, Figure 3): reference-model calibration alone contributes 0.09-0.19 AUC gain over plain loss attack (~0.5 AUC baseline); + progressive masking adds 2-3%; + robust sign-based subset aggregation adds the largest gain, 20-30% AUC; + adaptive weighting adds a final 3-5%
baselines_compared: SecMI (Duan et al. 2023, image-diffusion MIA adapted)
PIA (Kong et al. 2023, image-diffusion MIA adapted)
